module: Load data into a data warehouse

questions.question: Which three loading approaches does this module focus on for loading data into a Fabric warehouse?

options.A: Copy jobs in data pipelines, T-SQL statements, and Dataflow Gen2
options.B: Notebooks, REST APIs, and OneLake shortcuts
options.C: Spark jobs, Event streams, and KQL databases
options.D: Power BI dataflows, Azure Data Factory, and SSIS packages

questions.explanation: The module explores three common approaches: Copy jobs in data pipelines for wizard-driven ingestion, T-SQL statements like COPY and CTAS for code-based loading, and Dataflow Gen2 for visual Power Query transformations. Notebooks and REST APIs are mentioned as additional supported methods but are not the focus.

questions.question: What does a full load do to the target tables?

options.A: Updates only the rows that changed since the last load
options.B: Truncates and reloads all tables, replacing old data entirely
options.C: Appends new rows while preserving historical versions
options.D: Merges incoming rows based on a business key

questions.explanation: A full load truncates and reloads all tables so that old data is replaced entirely. It is simpler to implement because you don't track history, making it suitable for setting up a new warehouse or performing a complete refresh.

questions.question: Which statement correctly describes an incremental load?

options.A: It reloads every table from scratch on each run
options.B: It only works on dimension tables, never fact tables
options.C: It updates tables with only the changes since the last load and preserves history, but requires change-tracking mechanisms
options.D: It is always slower than a full load

questions.explanation: An incremental load updates tables with only the changes since the last load, preserving history. It is faster than a full load and suited to regular (daily, hourly) updates, but it requires change-tracking mechanisms in the source data.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of staging data before loading it into final warehouse tables?

options.A: To permanently archive raw source files
options.B: To provide a workspace to clean, transform, and validate incoming data, and to buffer the warehouse during large loads
options.C: To generate Power BI reports directly
options.D: To replace the need for dimension and fact tables

questions.explanation: Staging provides a workspace where staging tables, stored procedures, and functions clean, transform, and validate incoming data without affecting production tables. It also acts as a buffer that keeps the warehouse responsive while large loads process in the background.

questions.question: What is a business key (natural key)?

options.A: A system-generated integer with no business meaning
options.B: A key that comes from the source system and carries meaning, such as a product SKU or customer ID
options.C: A key used only to partition fact tables
options.D: A key that is always randomly generated by the warehouse

questions.explanation: A business key, also called a natural key, comes from the source system and carries meaning. Examples include product SKUs, customer IDs, and employee numbers. It lets you trace warehouse records back to their source.

questions.question: What is a surrogate key in a Fabric data warehouse?

options.A: A copy of the source system's primary key
options.B: A meaningful code such as a product SKU
options.C: A system-generated integer that uniquely identifies each warehouse record and has no business meaning
options.D: A foreign key that references the source database

questions.explanation: A surrogate key is a system-generated integer that uniquely identifies each warehouse record and has no business meaning. It protects the warehouse from source-system changes, keeping relationships intact even if a source reuses or modifies a code.

questions.question: In a star schema load, why are dimension tables loaded before fact tables?

options.A: Because fact tables reference dimension records through surrogate keys
options.B: Because dimension tables are always larger than fact tables
options.C: Because fact tables cannot use staging
options.D: Because dimensions never change once loaded

questions.explanation: Dimension tables are loaded first because fact tables reference them. Each fact row references dimension records through surrogate keys, so the dimension records (and their surrogate keys) must already exist before the fact load can look them up.

questions.question: Which SCD type adds a new row for each change and marks the old row as expired to retain full history?

options.A: Type 0
options.B: Type 1
options.C: Type 2
options.D: Type 3

questions.explanation: A Type 2 SCD adds a new row for each change and marks the old row as expired. It is used when you need full history, such as tracking a customer's address at the time of each sale.

questions.question: Which SCD type overwrites the existing value and keeps no history?

options.A: Type 1
options.B: Type 2
options.C: Type 4
options.D: Type 6

questions.explanation: A Type 1 SCD overwrites the existing value and keeps no history. It is appropriate when you don't need to track changes, for example when fixing a data-entry error.

questions.question: Which file formats can the T-SQL COPY statement load into a Fabric warehouse?

options.A: Only JSON files
options.B: CSV, Parquet, and other file formats
options.C: Only Excel workbooks
options.D: Only Delta tables

questions.explanation: The COPY statement loads data from external files including CSV, Parquet, and other formats. The files can be stored in Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2, Azure Blob Storage, or OneLake lakehouse folders.

questions.question: When loading files from a OneLake lakehouse folder with the COPY statement, what authentication is required?

options.A: A Shared Access Signature token
options.B: A Storage Account Key
options.C: No credential is needed; authentication uses your workspace identity automatically
options.D: A service principal secret

questions.explanation: For OneLake lakehouse folders, no credential is needed in the COPY statement because authentication uses your workspace identity automatically. SAS tokens or Storage Account Keys are only required for Azure storage accounts.

questions.question: What does the REJECTED_ROW_LOCATION option of the COPY statement do, and what file type does it apply to?

options.A: It deletes failed rows; applies to Parquet only
options.B: It sends failed rows to a separate storage location instead of failing the whole load; applies to CSV files only
options.C: It retries failed rows automatically; applies to all formats
options.D: It logs failed rows to the warehouse table; applies to JSON only

questions.explanation: REJECTED_ROW_LOCATION sends failed rows to a separate storage location instead of failing the entire load, letting you complete the bulk of the load and investigate problem rows separately. This option only applies to CSV files.

questions.question: Which T-SQL pattern creates a new table from a query result?

options.A: INSERT...SELECT
options.B: CREATE TABLE AS SELECT (CTAS)
options.C: MERGE
options.D: UPDATE...SET

questions.explanation: CREATE TABLE AS SELECT (CTAS) creates a new table from a query result and is used when you need a new table from transformed or combined data. INSERT...SELECT is used when the target table already exists.

questions.question: When you select Fabric Warehouse as a Dataflow Gen2 destination, which two update methods can you choose?

options.A: Merge and Upsert
options.B: Append and Replace
options.C: Truncate and Insert
options.D: Full and Incremental

questions.explanation: For a Fabric Warehouse destination in Dataflow Gen2 you choose between Append (add new rows to an existing table) and Replace (replace the entire table contents on each run).

questions.question: In Dataflow Gen2, what is the role of Copilot?

options.A: It schedules pipeline runs automatically
options.B: It generates Power Query transformation steps from plain-language instructions
options.C: It authenticates to Azure storage accounts
options.D: It converts the dataflow into a T-SQL stored procedure

questions.explanation: Copilot can generate Power Query transformation steps from plain-language instructions. You describe the transformation you want and Copilot adds the step automatically; you can inspect, revert, or build on the generated formula.

questions.question: A retail company is migrating to Microsoft Fabric. They first move all historical sales into the warehouse, then need to keep it current with daily changes from the source. Which loading approach best fits this overall pattern?

options.A: Use incremental loads for both the migration and daily updates
options.B: Use full loads for both the migration and daily updates
options.C: Use a full load during the initial migration, then switch to incremental loads for daily updates
options.D: Avoid staging and load directly into fact tables only

questions.explanation: Most warehouses use both patterns. You perform a full load during the initial migration to Fabric and then switch to incremental loads for ongoing daily updates, which is faster and preserves history.

questions.question: A data engineer must ensure that warehouse relationships stay intact even if the source system reuses or modifies a product code. What design choice addresses this requirement?

options.A: Use the source product code directly as the primary key on every dimension
options.B: Assign each warehouse record a system-generated surrogate key independent of the source code
options.C: Disable staging so codes can't be altered
options.D: Store only the most recent product code and overwrite it each load

questions.explanation: Surrogate keys are system-generated integers with no business meaning. They protect the warehouse from changes in source systems, so if a source reuses or modifies a product code, the surrogate key keeps the warehouse relationships intact.

questions.question: An analytics team must report on a customer's address as it was at the time of each historical sale, not their current address. Which SCD type should the customer dimension use?

options.A: Type 0
options.B: Type 1
options.C: Type 2
options.D: Type 3

questions.explanation: Type 2 SCD adds a new row for each change and expires the old row, preserving full history. This is exactly what's needed to link each sale to the customer's address at the time of the transaction rather than the current address.

questions.question: A dimension attribute was populated with a typo and must simply be corrected with no need to keep the old (wrong) value. Which SCD type is most appropriate?

options.A: Type 1, which overwrites the existing value
options.B: Type 2, which adds a new versioned row
options.C: Type 4, which moves the attribute to a separate table
options.D: Type 6, which combines Type 2 and Type 3

questions.explanation: Type 1 overwrites the existing value and keeps no history. It is the recommended choice when you don't need to track changes, such as correcting a data-entry error.

questions.question: An engineer wants the simplest wizard-driven way to move daily sales CSV files from Azure Blob Storage into a warehouse on a recurring schedule, without writing code. Which Fabric tool fits best?

options.A: A T-SQL COPY statement run manually each day
options.B: A Copy job in a data pipeline, scheduled to run automatically
options.C: A notebook with Spark code
options.D: An SSMS cross-database query

questions.explanation: A Copy job handles straightforward source-to-destination movement through a guided wizard and appears as an activity in a data pipeline, which can be scheduled to run automatically (for example, every morning). This is the low-code, wizard-driven approach.

questions.question: While configuring a Copy job, an engineer only wants to load rows that changed since the previous run. Which copy mode should they select?

options.A: Full copy
options.B: Incremental
options.C: Replace
options.D: Append

questions.explanation: In a Copy job, the Incremental copy mode loads only rows that changed since the previous run, while Full copy loads all source data on every run.

questions.question: During Copy job setup, an engineer needs to rename a few columns, change some data types, and exclude one column from the load. Which option provides this capability?

options.A: Choose data destination page
options.B: Edit column mapping
options.C: Review + save page
options.D: Schedule configuration

questions.explanation: The Edit column mapping option in the Copy job wizard lets you rename columns, change data types, or exclude columns from the load.

questions.question: After deploying a scheduled pipeline, a team keeps having downstream reports break because of failed loads they don't catch in time. Which Fabric capability helps them identify and troubleshoot failures before reports are affected?

options.A: The Edit column mapping screen
options.B: The pipeline's monitoring view for execution history, run status, and error details
options.C: The OneLake catalog preview
options.D: The Dataflow Gen2 Copilot pane

questions.explanation: Once a scheduled pipeline runs, you can monitor execution history, check run status, and review error details from the pipeline's monitoring view. This helps identify and troubleshoot failed loads before they affect downstream reports.

questions.question: An engineer needs to set a pipeline to run automatically every day at a specific time in a specific time zone. Where do they configure this?

options.A: By adding a REJECTED_ROW_LOCATION option
options.B: By selecting Schedule from the pipeline editor toolbar and setting start date, recurrence, and time zone
options.C: By choosing Replace in the data destination
options.D: By using three-part naming in a query

questions.explanation: After creating a pipeline, you select Schedule from the pipeline editor toolbar to set a recurring frequency. In the schedule configuration you set the start date, recurrence pattern (hourly, daily, weekly), and time zone. A schedule can also be set from Settings in the Home menu.

questions.question: A team wants to load a Fabric warehouse with daily transaction logs through Dataflow Gen2, accumulating records over time without losing prior data. Which warehouse update method should they choose?

options.A: Replace
options.B: Append
options.C: Truncate
options.D: Merge

questions.explanation: Append adds new rows to an existing table and is recommended for accumulating data over time, such as daily transaction logs. Replace would overwrite the entire table on each run.

questions.question: A product catalog in a Fabric warehouse is fully refreshed each run and should never retain stale rows from previous loads. Which Dataflow Gen2 warehouse update method fits?

options.A: Append
options.B: Replace
options.C: Incremental
options.D: Full copy

questions.explanation: Replace replaces the entire table contents on each run and is recommended for reference data that gets fully refreshed, such as a product catalog. Append would accumulate rather than refresh.

questions.question: An engineer configures a Dataflow Gen2 with an Azure Data Explorer (Kusto) destination and wants to use Replace. What is the outcome?

options.A: Replace works the same as for a Fabric Warehouse
options.B: Only Append is supported for KQL database and Azure Data Explorer destinations
options.C: Both Append and Replace are blocked for Kusto
options.D: Replace is allowed but Append is not

questions.explanation: Append is the only update method supported for KQL database and Azure Data Explorer destinations, so Replace is not available for those destinations.

questions.question: A data engineer builds a Dataflow Gen2, transforms data, and adds a destination, but the changes don't take effect when the dataflow runs. What step did they most likely miss?

options.A: They forgot to select Get data
options.B: They did not Publish the dataflow
options.C: They did not enable Copilot
options.D: They used Append instead of Replace

questions.explanation: Dataflow Gen2 auto-saves work as a draft while you build, but changes only take effect when you Publish. After publishing, you can run the dataflow manually or set it on a schedule.

questions.question: An engineer needs to combine rows from a sales_warehouse table and a social_lakehouse table into a brand-new table for analysis. Which T-SQL approach is most appropriate?

options.A: INSERT...SELECT into an existing table
options.B: CREATE TABLE AS SELECT (CTAS) using three-part naming to join across the assets
options.C: COPY INTO from a Blob Storage path
options.D: A Dataflow Gen2 with Append

questions.explanation: CTAS creates a new table from a query result and is used for new tables built from transformed or combined data. Because warehouses and lakehouses in the same workspace share a single SQL connection endpoint, you can join across them using three-part naming.

questions.question: An engineer wants to run cross-database queries against multiple Fabric warehouses from an external client. Which statement is accurate?

options.A: Cross-database queries are only possible inside the Fabric portal
options.B: You can run cross-database queries from SQL Server Management Studio or other SQL clients connected to the Fabric workspace
options.C: Each warehouse requires a separate connection endpoint that cannot be queried together
options.D: Cross-asset queries require a Dataflow Gen2

questions.explanation: You can run cross-database queries from SQL Server Management Studio or other SQL client tools that connect to your Fabric workspace, because all warehouses in the same workspace share a single SQL connection endpoint. In the portal's SQL editor you can also add warehouses to the object explorer.

questions.question: An engineer must load many CSV files that all share the same structure from a single Blob Storage folder in one statement. What COPY feature enables this?

options.A: The REJECTED_ROW_LOCATION option
options.B: A wildcard such as *.csv in the file path
options.C: Setting FILE_TYPE to Parquet
options.D: Three-part naming

questions.explanation: The COPY statement supports a wildcard (for example, *.csv) so you can load multiple files in one statement as long as they share the same structure. You can also list multiple paths from the same storage account separated by commas.

questions.question: A pipeline needs to do more than a single copy: it must copy files, then run a stored procedure, then refresh a dataflow, all chained in one workflow. Which Fabric construct supports this?

options.A: A single COPY INTO statement
options.B: A data pipeline containing multiple activities such as Copy, Dataflow, and stored procedure calls
options.C: A standalone Dataflow Gen2
options.D: A surrogate key lookup

questions.explanation: A data pipeline can contain multiple activities (Copy, Dataflow, stored procedure calls, and more) chained together into a single workflow that moves data, applies transformations, and schedules recurring loads without writing code.

questions.question: An engineer is deciding between Dataflow Gen2 and a T-SQL COPY statement to ingest CSV files. The team has no coding skills but needs visual, step-by-step transformations before landing data. Which tool fits best?

options.A: T-SQL COPY, because it is wizard-driven
options.B: Dataflow Gen2, a visual Power Query interface where you connect, transform, and send results to a destination without writing code
options.C: Cross-database CTAS
options.D: REST API ingestion

questions.explanation: Dataflow Gen2 is a visual Power Query interface where you connect to sources, apply transformations, and send results to a destination, all without writing code. This suits teams that need visual transformations and lack coding skills.

questions.question: An engineer wants to load a small CSV file straight from their local computer into a Dataflow Gen2 for cleaning and reshaping. How can they import it?

options.A: Only by first uploading it to Azure Blob Storage
options.B: By selecting Get data and choosing Import from a Text/CSV file, then browsing to the local file
options.C: By using COPY INTO with a SAS token
options.D: By creating a Copy job

questions.explanation: In Dataflow Gen2 you select Get data to choose a source. For file-based sources you can upload directly from your local computer; for a CSV you select Import from a Text/CSV file and browse to your file. After importing you can clean, reshape, rename columns, and add calculated columns.

questions.question: During a fact table load, staged fact data contains source business keys but the fact table must store surrogate keys. What must the loading logic do?

options.A: Insert the business keys directly into the fact table
options.B: Look up the matching surrogate key in each dimension table for every business key
options.C: Truncate the dimension tables before loading facts
options.D: Generate new surrogate keys for the fact rows

questions.explanation: Staged fact data contains business keys from source systems, so the loading logic looks up the matching surrogate key in each dimension table. When dimensions use Type 2 SCD, the logic must also match the correct version of the dimension record.

questions.question: A warehouse uses Type 2 SCD on its customer dimension. When loading facts, why isn't it always enough to simply match on the customer business key?

options.A: Because business keys are not stored in staging
options.B: Because multiple versions of the same customer exist, so you must match the correct version (often the most recent, sometimes by validity dates)
options.C: Because facts cannot reference Type 2 dimensions
options.D: Because surrogate keys are removed during Type 2 loads

questions.explanation: With Type 2 SCD, each change creates a new versioned row, so multiple versions of the same business key exist. Fact loading must match the correct version of the dimension record. In many cases you retrieve the most recent version; sometimes you match based on validity dates.

questions.question: An engineer must load Parquet files from Azure Blob Storage with the COPY statement and wants Fabric to determine the format automatically. What can they do?

options.A: They must always set FILE_TYPE = 'Parquet'
options.B: They can omit the FILE_TYPE parameter because Fabric infers the format from the file extension
options.C: They must convert the files to CSV first
options.D: They must use REJECTED_ROW_LOCATION

questions.explanation: For Parquet files you can omit the FILE_TYPE parameter because Fabric infers the format from the file extension. A credential is still needed for Azure storage accounts.

questions.question: An engineer is staging data and wants to apply business rules and data type conversions without touching production tables, while also keeping the warehouse responsive during a large load. Which benefit of staging does this describe?

options.A: Staging eliminates the need for surrogate keys
options.B: Staging both provides a place to apply rules and conversions safely and acts as a buffer that keeps the warehouse responsive
options.C: Staging automatically schedules pipelines
options.D: Staging converts dimensions to Type 1

questions.explanation: Staging serves two purposes: it gives a place to apply business rules and data type conversions without affecting production tables, and it acts as a buffer that keeps the warehouse responsive while large data loads process in the background.

questions.question: A team must minimize implementation complexity when first standing up a brand-new warehouse and does not yet need to track historical change. Which load type is simpler and why?

options.A: Incremental load, because it tracks history automatically
options.B: Full load, because you don't track history
options.C: Incremental load, because it requires no change-tracking
options.D: Full load, because it preserves all prior versions

questions.explanation: A full load is simpler to implement because you don't track history; it truncates and reloads all tables. It is well suited to setting up a new warehouse or performing a complete refresh.

questions.question: Contoso Retail is migrating analytics to Microsoft Fabric. Sales data arrives as daily CSV exports in Azure Blob Storage; the product catalog comes from an on-premises SQL Server; and customer activity logs stream into OneLake. The team must (1) perform a one-time historical load and then daily incremental updates, (2) stage data to apply business rules before production tables, (3) load dimensions before facts using surrogate keys, (4) track each customer's address at the time of every sale, and (5) fully refresh the product catalog reference table each run via a no-code visual tool. Which combination of choices correctly satisfies ALL of these requirements?

options.A: Full load for migration then incremental for daily updates; stage data first; load dimensions before facts and look up surrogate keys; use Type 2 SCD on the customer dimension; and use Dataflow Gen2 with the Replace update method for the product catalog
options.B: Incremental load for both migration and daily updates; skip staging; load facts before dimensions; use Type 1 SCD on the customer dimension; and use Dataflow Gen2 with Append for the product catalog
options.C: Full load for both migration and daily updates; stage data first; load dimensions and facts in any order; use Type 0 on the customer dimension; and use Replace for the product catalog
options.D: Full load for migration then incremental for updates; stage data first; load dimensions before facts; use Type 1 SCD on the customer dimension; and use Append for the product catalog

questions.explanation: A full load fits the one-time historical migration and incremental fits daily updates. Staging applies business rules before production. Dimensions load before facts so fact rows can look up surrogate keys. Type 2 SCD preserves the customer's address at the time of each sale. The product catalog is reference data fully refreshed each run, which matches the Replace update method in Dataflow Gen2 (a no-code visual tool). Option D fails because Type 1 would overwrite address history and Append would not fully refresh the catalog; B and C violate load order, staging, or SCD requirements.

questions.question:
Consider this COPY statement loading from Azure Blob Storage:

COPY INTO dbo.Sales
FROM 'https://acct.blob.core.windows.net/data/2026/*.csv'
WITH (
  FILE_TYPE = 'CSV',
  CREDENTIAL=(IDENTITY='Shared Access Signature', SECRET='<token>'),
  FIELDTERMINATOR = '|'
);

What is the effect of the FROM path and FIELDTERMINATOR settings?

options.A: It loads exactly one file named *.csv and splits fields on commas
options.B: It loads all .csv files in the 2026 folder (matched by the wildcard) and splits fields on the pipe character
options.C: It loads only Parquet files and ignores the terminator
options.D: It fails because a wildcard cannot be used with CSV

questions.explanation: The wildcard *.csv matches all CSV files in the folder that share the same structure, loading them in one statement. FIELDTERMINATOR = '|' tells COPY that fields are delimited by the pipe character rather than a comma.

questions.question:
An engineer writes:

COPY INTO dbo.Orders_parquet
FROM 'https://acct.blob.core.windows.net/data/orders/*.parquet'
WITH (
  CREDENTIAL=(IDENTITY='Shared Access Signature', SECRET='<token>')
);

Why does this statement work even though FILE_TYPE is not specified?

options.A: COPY always defaults to Parquet
options.B: Fabric infers the format from the .parquet file extension
options.C: FILE_TYPE is only required for OneLake sources
options.D: The wildcard forces Parquet mode

questions.explanation: For Parquet files you can omit the FILE_TYPE parameter because Fabric infers the format from the file extension. The credential is still required because the source is an Azure Blob Storage account.

questions.question: A team loads CSV files and adds REJECTED_ROW_LOCATION to the COPY statement. During the load, 12 of 1,000,000 rows have malformed data. What happens?

options.A: The entire load fails and no rows are inserted
options.B: All 1,000,000 rows are inserted, including the 12 malformed ones
options.C: The 12 bad rows are sent to the rejected-row storage location and the remaining valid rows still load
options.D: The 12 bad rows are retried until they succeed

questions.explanation: REJECTED_ROW_LOCATION sends failed rows to a separate storage location instead of failing the whole load, so the bulk of valid rows load successfully and the 12 rejected rows can be investigated separately. This option applies to CSV files only.

questions.question:
Consider this cross-asset T-SQL run in a Fabric workspace:

CREATE TABLE [analysis_wh].[dbo].[combined]
AS
SELECT s.*, c.region
FROM [sales_wh].[dbo].[sales] s
INNER JOIN [crm_lake].[dbo].[customers] c
  ON s.cust_id = c.cust_id;

What does this statement accomplish and why is it valid?

options.A: It inserts into an existing 'combined' table; valid because the table already exists
options.B: It creates a new 'combined' table from the joined result; valid because all warehouses/lakehouses in the same workspace share one SQL endpoint, allowing three-part naming
options.C: It fails because you cannot join a warehouse to a lakehouse
options.D: It only works from SSMS, never inside the Fabric portal

questions.explanation: This is a CTAS statement that creates a new table from a query joining a warehouse and a lakehouse. It is valid because all warehouses (and lakehouse SQL endpoints) in the same workspace share a single SQL connection endpoint, so you can reference them with three-part naming like databases on a SQL Server instance.

questions.question: An engineer must add new rows to an EXISTING dbo.FactSales table that is already populated, without recreating it. Which T-SQL pattern is correct?

options.A: CREATE TABLE AS SELECT, because it always appends to existing tables
options.B: INSERT...SELECT, because the target table already exists and you are adding data to it
options.C: COPY INTO, because it only works on existing tables
options.D: TRUNCATE TABLE followed by CREATE TABLE AS SELECT

questions.explanation: INSERT...SELECT inserts rows into an existing table and is used when the target already exists and you're adding data. CTAS instead creates a brand-new table from a query result and cannot target an existing table.

questions.question:
Review this Type 2 SCD logic for a Dim_Products table:

IF EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM Dim_Products
          WHERE SourceKey=@ProductID AND IsActive='True')
BEGIN
  UPDATE Dim_Products
    SET ValidTo=GETDATE(), IsActive='False'
  WHERE SourceKey=@ProductID AND IsActive='True';
END
ELSE
BEGIN
  INSERT INTO Dim_Products (SourceKey, ProductName, StartDate, EndDate, IsActive)
  VALUES (@ProductID, @ProductName, GETDATE(), '9999-12-31', 'True');
END

For an EXISTING active product whose name changed, which branch executes and what is its immediate effect?

options.A: The ELSE branch runs and inserts a brand-new first version
options.B: The IF branch runs and expires the current active version by setting ValidTo and IsActive='False'
options.C: Neither branch runs because the product already exists
options.D: Both branches run, deleting the old row

questions.explanation: Because an active record with that SourceKey already exists, EXISTS returns true and the IF branch runs. It expires the current version by setting ValidTo=GETDATE() and IsActive='False'. (In a complete Type 2 process, a new active version would then be inserted; the ELSE branch only handles a brand-new product.)

questions.question: In the same Type 2 SCD procedure, the ELSE branch inserts EndDate='9999-12-31' and IsActive='True' for a brand-new product. Why is the date '9999-12-31' used?

options.A: It is a required Fabric system constant
options.B: It marks the row as the current, non-expired version with an open-ended validity far in the future
options.C: It schedules the row to be deleted on that date
options.D: It tells COPY to reject the row

questions.explanation: Setting EndDate to a far-future value like '9999-12-31' together with IsActive='True' marks the row as the current, open-ended version. When the product later changes, that row is expired (ValidTo/IsActive updated) and a new current version is added.

questions.question:
Examine this fact-load surrogate key lookup:

INSERT INTO dbo.FactSales
SELECT
  (SELECT MAX(ProductKey) FROM dbo.DimProduct
   WHERE ProductAlternateKey = stg.ProductID) AS ProductKey,
  stg.OrderNumber, stg.OrderQuantity, stg.SalesAmount
FROM dbo.StageSales AS stg;

Given Type 2 dimensions with incrementing surrogate keys, why is MAX(ProductKey) used?

options.A: To count how many product versions exist
options.B: To retrieve the most recently added (current) version's surrogate key, since surrogate keys increment with each new version
options.C: To pick the smallest, oldest surrogate key
options.D: To generate a brand-new surrogate key for the fact row

questions.explanation: The example assumes dimension records have incrementing surrogate keys, so MAX() retrieves the most recently added version for each matching business key. This links each staged fact (matched by business key, e.g., ProductAlternateKey = stg.ProductID) to the current dimension version.

questions.question: An engineer attempts a COPY statement loading CSV files from a OneLake lakehouse folder and omits the CREDENTIAL parameter entirely. What is the result?

options.A: The statement fails because every COPY requires a SAS token
options.B: The statement succeeds because OneLake lakehouse folders authenticate automatically using your workspace identity
options.C: The statement succeeds but only for Parquet files
options.D: The statement requires a Storage Account Key instead

questions.explanation: For OneLake lakehouse folders no credential is needed because authentication uses your workspace identity automatically. A CREDENTIAL (SAS or Storage Account Key) is only required when loading from Azure storage accounts.
